questions.question:
The Boolean function in sum of products form where K-map is given below (figure) is _______

K-map (variables B and C, with A as a parameter):

         C
     B | 0  | 1
    ---+----+----
     0 | 1  | 0
    ---+----+----
     1 | A' | A
questions.to_find: Minimal SOP Boolean expression from the given 2-variable K-map

understand.plain: A K-map with variables B and C is given where the cell entries involve another variable A. We need to read off the minterms and group them to get the minimal Sum of Products expression. The cell at B=0,C=0 is 1 (constant), at B=1,C=0 is A' and at B=1,C=1 is A. We express the output F in terms of A, B, C.

keywords.explain: A Boolean expression written as OR of AND terms, e.g. AB + CD.
Each cell of a K-map holds the function value for that combination of input variables; entries can be 0, 1, or even expressions in other variables.
keywords.example: Cell B=1,C=0 holds A', meaning for B=1,C=0,A=0 the function is 1 and for B=1,C=0,A=1 it is 0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='200' viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='340' height='200' rx='8' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='100' y='35' class='lbl ink'>C=0</text><text x='200' y='35' class='lbl ink'>C=1</text><text x='30' y='80' class='lbl ink'>B=0</text><text x='30' y='140' class='lbl ink'>B=1</text><rect x='80' y='50' width='100' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='180' y='50' width='100' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>0</text><rect x='80' y='110' width='100' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A'</text><rect x='180' y='110' width='100' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='170' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>B=0,C=0 is always 1 (gives B'C' term)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 2-variable K-map with rows B=0,B=1 and columns C=0,C=1. Entries: (0,0)=1, (0,1)=0, (1,0)=A', (1,1)=A.

given.aim: Find the minimal SOP expression for a 3-variable Boolean function F(A,B,C) from the given K-map.

terms.meaning: Output is 1 regardless of A for this combination.
terms.example: Contributes minterm m0 (A=0) and m4 (A=1) — both 1.
terms.connects: Since it is 1 for all A, it gives product term B'C'.

terms.meaning: Output is A' for B=1, C=0, meaning 1 only when A=0.
Output is A for B=1, C=1, meaning 1 only when A=1.

given.plan: List all minterms where F=1: (B=0,C=0) for all A gives B'C'. (B=1,C=0,A=0) gives A'BC'. (B=1,C=1,A=1) gives ABC. Then simplify: B'C' cannot merge with A'BC' to give A'C' (since A'BC' is the only A'=1 occurrence in the BC=10 column). Group B'C' and A'BC' together: they share C=0 and A=0 for the second, but B'C' covers both A values. So: F = B'C' + A'BC' + ABC. We can factor: B'C' + A'BC' = C'(B' + A'B) = C'(A' + B') ... let us simplify differently. B'C' + A'BC' = C'(B' + A'B) = C'(A'+B') = A'C' + B'C'. So F = A'C' + B'C' + ABC.

steps.title: List all minterms (cells where F=1)
steps.formula_raw: F = sum of minterms where cell value = 1
steps.apply:
Cell (B=0,C=0): value=1 for A=0 and A=1 -> minterms: A=0,B=0,C=0 and A=1,B=0,C=0.
Cell (B=1,C=0): value=A' -> F=1 when A=0 -> minterm: A=0,B=1,C=0.
Cell (B=1,C=1): value=A -> F=1 when A=1 -> minterm: A=1,B=1,C=1.
Cell (B=0,C=1): value=0 -> no minterms.
Minterms: (0,0,0), (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (1,1,1)
steps.note: The cell entries A' and A encode the value of F in terms of the third variable.

steps.title: Group minterms and simplify
steps.formula_raw: Group pairs/quads that differ in exactly one variable
steps.apply:
Group1: (A=0,B=0,C=0) and (A=1,B=0,C=0) -> differ in A -> product: B'C'
Group2: (A=0,B=0,C=0) and (A=0,B=1,C=0) -> differ in B -> product: A'C'
Group3: (A=1,B=1,C=1) alone -> product: ABC
Using Group1 and Group2 to cover all minterms:
- (1,0,0) covered by Group1 (B'C')
- (0,0,0) covered by Group1 and Group2
- (0,1,0) covered by Group2 (A'C')
- (1,1,1) covered by Group3 (ABC)
So minimal SOP: F = B'C' + A'C' + ABC
steps.note: We prefer the grouping that minimizes literals. B'C' + A'C' can be written as (A'+B')C' but SOP form keeps them separate.

questions.formula_note: K-map grouping: cells differing in exactly one variable can be merged to eliminate that variable from the product term.

questions.question:
Implement a circuit having the following output expression using an inverter and a NAND gate:

Z = A' + B' + C
questions.answer: Z = NAND(A, B, C'). Use one inverter on C to get C', then feed A, B, C' into a 3-input NAND gate.
questions.to_find: Circuit implementation of Z = A' + B' + C using only one inverter and one NAND gate

understand.plain: We need to realize Z = A' + B' + C using just one inverter and one NAND gate. By De Morgan's theorem, a NAND gate with inputs X, Y, Z produces (XYZ)' = X' + Y' + Z'. If we feed A, B, C' into a NAND gate we get (A . B . C')' = A' + B' + C, which is exactly Z. So we use one inverter on C to produce C', then one 3-input NAND gate.

keywords.explain: A universal gate that computes NOT(AND). For 3 inputs: NAND(X,Y,Z) = (XYZ)' = X' + Y' + Z' by De Morgan.
(X1 . X2 . ... . Xn)' = X1' + X2' + ... + Xn'. Converts NAND to OR-of-complements.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' viewBox='0 0 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='400' height='160' rx='8' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='50' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='20' y='110' class='lbl ink'>C</text><line x1='40' y1='48' x2='160' y2='48' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='78' x2='160' y2='78' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='108' x2='100' y2='108' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='100' y='95' width='40' height='26' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='120' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>INV</text><line x1='140' y1='108' x2='160' y2='108' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='148' y='104' class='sub mut'>C'</text><rect x='160' y='30' width='80' height='100' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='200' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND</text><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='320' y2='80' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='325' y='85' class='lbl ink'>Z</text><text x='50' y='150' class='sub mut'>Z = NAND(A, B, C') = A' + B' + C</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A circuit with inputs A, B going directly to a 3-input NAND gate; C goes through an inverter to produce C', which is the third input to the NAND gate. The NAND output is Z = A' + B' + C.

given.aim: Implement Z = A' + B' + C using exactly one inverter and one NAND gate.

terms.meaning: The target output expression — an OR of complemented and uncomplemented variables.
terms.connects: This expression matches the De Morgan form of a NAND gate if one input is pre-complemented.

terms.meaning: A NOT gate; produces complement of its input.
terms.connects: Feeding C' to NAND allows the NAND to handle the non-complemented C term.

terms.meaning: NAND(X,Y,Z) = (XYZ)' = X'+Y'+Z' by De Morgan's law.
terms.connects: Directly gives us Z when inputs are A, B, C'.

given.plan: Apply De Morgan's law: Z = A' + B' + C = A' + B' + (C')' = (A . B . C')'. So NAND(A, B, C') = Z. Pre-complement C with the inverter, then connect A, B, C' to a 3-input NAND gate.

steps.title: Apply De Morgan's theorem to match NAND form
steps.apply:
Z = A' + B' + C
  = A' + B' + (C')'
  = (A . B . C')'
This is exactly the output of a 3-input NAND gate with inputs A, B, C'.
steps.note: The key insight: replace C with (C')' so all terms fit the De Morgan pattern.

steps.title: Design the circuit
steps.formula_raw: NAND(A, B, C') = (A . B . C')' = A' + B' + C
steps.apply:
Step 1: Connect C to the inverter -> output is C'.
Step 2: Connect A, B, and C' to a 3-input NAND gate.
Step 3: Output of NAND gate = (A . B . C')' = A' + B' + C = Z.
Total gates used: 1 inverter + 1 NAND gate.
steps.note: This uses exactly the allowed resources: one inverter and one NAND gate.

solution.result: Connect C through an inverter to get C', then feed A, B, C' into a 3-input NAND gate. The output Z = NAND(A, B, C') = A' + B' + C.

questions.formula_note: De Morgan's theorem: (X1.X2...Xn)' = X1'+X2'+...+Xn', allowing NAND gate to realize OR-of-complements form.

questions.question:
What is the equivalent minimal Boolean expression (in sum of products form) for the Karnaugh map given below?

K-map (4 variables: AB vs CD):

      AB
CD  | 00 | 01 | 11 | 10
----+----+----+----+----
 00 |  1 |    |    |  1
----+----+----+----+----
 01 |    |  1 |  1 |   
----+----+----+----+----
 11 |    |  1 |  1 |   
----+----+----+----+----
 10 |  1 |    |    |  1
questions.to_find: Minimal SOP expression from the 4-variable K-map

understand.plain: A 4-variable K-map (variables A,B,C,D) is given with 1s in specific cells. We need to identify groups of 1s (pairs, quads, octets) that cover all minterms with minimal terms. Looking at the pattern, 1s appear in the corners and in specific inner cells showing a clear pattern based on B and D.

keywords.explain: A 4x4 grid where rows represent two variables (CD) and columns represent two variables (AB), arranged in Gray code order.
keywords.example: Cell at row CD=01, col AB=01 corresponds to A=0,B=1,C=0,D=1.

keywords.explain: Grouping 4 cells that form a rectangle (or wrap around) in a K-map eliminates 2 variables.
keywords.example: 4 corner cells in a 4-variable K-map form a valid quad.

class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='185' y='152' width='50' height='45' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='235' y='152' width='50' height='45' understand.visual_alt: 4-variable K-map with AB on columns (00,01,11,10) and CD on rows (00,01,11,10). Ones at: (CD=00,AB=00), (CD=00,AB=10), (CD=01,AB=01), (CD=01,AB=11), (CD=11,AB=01), (CD=11,AB=11), (CD=10,AB=00), (CD=10,AB=10).

given.aim: Find the minimal SOP expression from a 4-variable K-map with the given 1 positions.

terms.meaning: Cells where B=0 and D=0: AB=00 with CD=00 and CD=10, plus AB=10 with CD=00 and CD=10.
terms.connects: These 4 minterms form a quad that simplifies to B'D'.

terms.meaning: Cells where B=1 and D=1: AB=01 with CD=01 and CD=11, plus AB=11 with CD=01 and CD=11.
terms.connects: These 4 minterms form a quad that simplifies to BD.

terms.meaning: K-map rows/columns are ordered 00,01,11,10 so adjacent cells differ by one bit.
terms.example: Columns 00 and 10 are adjacent in the K-map (wrap-around), both have B=0.
terms.connects: Enables recognizing that corner cells in columns 00 and 10 share B=0.

given.plan: Identify all 1s in the map. Observe that 1s occur exactly when (B=0 AND D=0) OR (B=1 AND D=1), i.e., when B XNOR D = 1. Group them as two quads: one quad for B=0,D=0 and another for B=1,D=1.

steps.title: List all minterms (decode K-map positions)
steps.formula_raw: Minterm at row CD, col AB: variables A,B from AB; C,D from CD
steps.note: Total 8 minterms. The pattern: B=0,D=0 OR B=1,D=1.

steps.title: Form groups and extract SOP terms
steps.apply:
Group 1 (B=0, D=0): {m0, m2, m8, m10}
  A varies (0 and 1), C varies (0 and 1), B=0, D=0
  Simplified term: B'D'

Group 2 (B=1, D=1): {m5, m7, m13, m15}
  A varies (0 and 1), C varies (0 and 1), B=1, D=1
  Simplified term: BD

Both groups cover all 8 minterms. No minterms left uncovered.
Minimal SOP: F = B'D' + BD
steps.note: Each group is a quad (4 cells) that eliminates A and C, keeping only B and D.

questions.formula_note: K-map quad grouping: 4 adjacent 1-cells in a rectangle (including wrap-around) reduce to a 2-literal product term.

questions.question:
What is the equivalent Boolean expression in product-of-sums form for the Karnaugh map given in Fig?

K-map (4 variables: AB vs CD):

      AB
CD  | 00 | 01 | 11 | 10
----+----+----+----+----
 00 |    |  1 |  1 |   
----+----+----+----+----
 01 |  1 |    |    |  1
----+----+----+----+----
 11 |  1 |    |    |  1
----+----+----+----+----
 10 |    |  1 |  1 |   

A. BD' + B'D
B. (B + C' + D)(B' + C + D')
C. (B + D)(B' + D')
D. (B + D')(B' + D)
questions.to_find: Minimal POS Boolean expression from the 4-variable K-map

understand.plain: We have a 4-variable K-map and need the Product of Sums (POS) form. For POS, we group the 0s in the K-map (instead of 1s), find the SOP of the 0-cells, and complement the result. Alternatively, we can observe the pattern of 1s and 0s directly. The 0s form groups that give sum terms.

keywords.explain: A Boolean expression written as AND of OR terms (sum terms), e.g. (A+B)(C+D').
To get POS, group the 0s in the K-map. Each group of 0s gives a sum term (the complement of what SOP grouping gives for 1s). Then AND all sum terms.
keywords.example: A quad of 0s where B=0,D=0 gives sum term (B+D).

understand.visual_alt: 4-variable K-map for question 6.18.6. Ones at: (CD=00,AB=01), (CD=00,AB=11), (CD=01,AB=00), (CD=01,AB=10), (CD=11,AB=00), (CD=11,AB=10), (CD=10,AB=01), (CD=10,AB=11). Zeros at the remaining 8 cells.

given.aim: Find the minimal POS expression by grouping the 0-cells of the K-map.

terms.meaning: Cells where F=0, used to build sum terms for POS form.
terms.connects: Grouping 0s gives maxterms whose AND forms the POS.

terms.meaning: For a group of 0s, the sum term is formed by: take variables constant in the group; if a variable is 0 in the group, it appears uncomplemented in the sum; if 1, it appears complemented.
terms.example: Group where B=0,D=1: sum term is (B+D').
terms.connects: Opposite sign convention from SOP grouping of 1s.

terms.meaning: The 1s show F = B'D' + BD (from previous analysis of this pattern), which is the XNOR of B and D.
terms.connects: POS of F can be derived as: F=0 when B!=D, giving groups B=0,D=1 (sum: B+D') and B=1,D=0 (sum: B'+D).

given.plan: Identify 0-cells. Group them into quads. Each quad gives a sum term (variable=0 in group -> literal appears uncomplemented; variable=1 in group -> literal appears complemented). AND the sum terms for POS.

steps.title: Identify 0-cells and group them
steps.formula_raw: POS: group 0s; sum term sign rule: 0 -> uncomplemented, 1 -> complemented
steps.apply:
Zero cells (F=0): occurs when B!=D:
- B=0, D=1: (AB=00,CD=01), (AB=00,CD=11), (AB=10,CD=01), (AB=10,CD=11) -> 4 cells
- B=1, D=0: (AB=01,CD=00), (AB=11,CD=00), (AB=01,CD=10), (AB=11,CD=10) -> 4 cells

Group1 (B=0, D=1): quad covering all A and C values with B=0,D=1
  B=0 -> B appears uncomplemented: B
  D=1 -> D appears complemented: D'
  Sum term: (B + D')

Group2 (B=1, D=0): quad covering all A and C values with B=1,D=0
  B=1 -> B appears complemented: B'
  D=0 -> D appears uncomplemented: D
  Sum term: (B' + D)
steps.note: A and C are free (vary over 0 and 1) within each group, so they are eliminated from the sum terms.

steps.title: Form the POS expression
steps.note: This expression is the XNOR of B and D in POS form: F = B XNOR D.

solution.result: F = (B + D')(B' + D), which is option D.

questions.formula_note: POS from K-map: group 0-cells; each group gives a sum term where variables that are 0 in the group appear uncomplemented and variables that are 1 appear complemented.
